rules.title: Kernel pointer addresses hidden from unprivileged users
rules.rationale: kernel.kptr_restrict=0 exposes raw kernel pointers via /proc and dmesg, defeating KASLR and giving exploit developers the addresses they need.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=2 and persist in /etc/sysctl.d/99-hardening.conf

rules.title: dmesg restricted to privileged users
rules.rationale: The kernel log leaks pointer values, hardware details and oops traces that assist local privilege escalation.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.dmesg_restrict=1

rules.title: Unprivileged BPF disabled
rules.rationale: Unprivileged eBPF has been the entry point for multiple verifier privilege-escalation exploits (e.g. CVE-2020-8835, CVE-2021-3490). Value 1 disables it until reboot; 2 allows re-enabling.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1

rules.title: BPF JIT hardening enabled for all users
rules.rationale: JIT hardening (constant blinding) mitigates JIT-spray attacks against the BPF JIT compiler.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w net.core.bpf_jit_harden=2

rules.title: kexec of new kernels disabled
rules.rationale: kexec allows replacing the running kernel, bypassing Secure Boot and lockdown; disabling it closes a persistence and integrity-bypass path.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.kexec_load_disabled=1 (one-way switch)

rules.title: ptrace scope restricted
rules.rationale: Unrestricted ptrace lets any compromised process read memory and inject into other processes of the same user (credential theft, SSH-agent hijack).
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.yama.ptrace_scope=1 (or higher)

rules.title: perf_event access restricted
rules.rationale: perf exposes side channels and has a history of exploitable bugs reachable by unprivileged users.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.perf_event_paranoid=3 (2 on kernels without the =3 patch)

rules.title: Magic SysRq disabled or restricted
rules.rationale: SysRq allows console users to crash, reboot or dump memory of the machine. Servers rarely need it.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.sysrq=0

rules.title: Address space layout randomization fully enabled
rules.rationale: randomize_va_space=2 randomizes stack, mmap, VDSO and heap; anything lower makes userspace exploitation dramatically easier.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2

rules.title: Unprivileged userfaultfd disabled
rules.rationale: userfaultfd lets attackers pause kernel faults at will, turning hard race conditions into reliable exploits; unprivileged access is rarely needed.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w vm.unprivileged_userfaultfd=0

rules.title: Symlink protection enabled
rules.rationale: Prevents classic /tmp symlink race attacks by restricting symlink following in sticky world-writable directories.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w fs.protected_symlinks=1

rules.title: Hardlink protection enabled
rules.rationale: Prevents privilege escalation via hardlinks to files the attacker cannot write.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w fs.protected_hardlinks=1

rules.title: FIFO protection in world-writable directories
rules.rationale: Blocks data-spoofing attacks via attacker-created FIFOs in sticky directories.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w fs.protected_fifos=2

rules.title: Regular-file protection in world-writable directories
rules.rationale: Blocks unintended writes through attacker-created files in sticky directories.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w fs.protected_regular=2

rules.title: setuid core dumps disabled
rules.rationale: Core dumps of setuid binaries can leak privileged memory (password hashes, keys) to unprivileged users.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w fs.suid_dumpable=0

rules.title: TTY line discipline autoloading disabled
rules.rationale: Autoloading obscure line-discipline modules on demand has enabled multiple privilege-escalation exploits (e.g. n_hdlc CVE-2017-2636).
rules.remediation: sysctl -w dev.tty.ldisc_autoload=0

rules.title: Minimum mmap address enforced
rules.rationale: Prevents userspace from mapping page zero, which turns kernel NULL-pointer dereference bugs into privilege escalation.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w vm.mmap_min_addr=65536

rules.title: io_uring restricted (kernel >= 6.6)
rules.rationale: io_uring has produced a steady stream of exploitable kernel bugs; Google disabled it fleet-wide in production. On kernels that support it, restrict creation to privileged users.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w kernel.io_uring_disabled=2 (or 1 to limit to CAP_SYS_ADMIN)

rules.title: TCP SYN cookies enabled
rules.rationale: SYN cookies keep the host reachable under SYN-flood denial-of-service attacks.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1

rules.title: ICMP redirects not accepted
rules.rationale: Accepting ICMP redirects lets on-path attackers alter the routing table for man-in-the-middle attacks.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects=0 net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects=0

rules.title: Source-routed packets not accepted
rules.rationale: Source routing allows attackers to dictate packet paths and bypass network security controls.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route=0

rules.title: Unprivileged user namespaces surface (informational)
rules.rationale: Unprivileged user namespaces are the reachability primitive for a large share of recent kernel LPEs, but disabling them breaks rootless containers, Flatpak and browser sandboxes. Review whether your workload needs them.
rules.remediation: If not required: sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0, or restrict via AppArmor/seccomp per workload.

rules.title: Module loading permanently disabled (strict environments)
rules.rationale: kernel.modules_disabled=1 is a one-way switch that blocks all further module loading — the strongest anti-rootkit control, suitable for appliances and locked-down hosts after boot completes.
rules.remediation: After all required modules are loaded: sysctl -w kernel.modules_disabled=1

rules.title: ICMPv6 redirects not accepted
rules.rationale: Accepting ICMPv6 redirects lets on-path attackers alter IPv6 routing for man-in-the-middle attacks — the IPv6 counterpart of the IPv4 redirect rule; hardening only IPv4 leaves the same attack open over IPv6.
rules.remediation: sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects=0 net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects=0

rules.title: CPU vulnerability mitigations not globally disabled
rules.rationale: mitigations=off disables every hardware vulnerability mitigation (Spectre, Meltdown, MDS, ...), re-exposing the host to well-understood cross-boundary attacks.
rules.remediation: Remove mitigations=off from the kernel command line and reboot.

rules.title: KASLR not disabled at boot
rules.rationale: nokaslr places the kernel at a fixed address, removing the primary probabilistic defense against kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Remove nokaslr from the kernel command line.

rules.title: SMEP/SMAP not disabled at boot
rules.rationale: nosmep/nosmap let the kernel execute or access attacker-controlled userspace memory — a decisive advantage for kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Remove nosmep and nosmap from the kernel command line.

rules.title: Page-table isolation not disabled
rules.rationale: nopti / pti=off disables Meltdown protection on affected CPUs.
rules.remediation: Remove nopti / pti=off from the kernel command line.

rules.title: Slab merging disabled (slab_nomerge)
rules.rationale: Merged slab caches make heap-spray and cross-cache attacks easier; slab_nomerge keeps caches separate at modest memory cost.
rules.remediation: Add slab_nomerge to the kernel command line.

rules.title: Memory zeroed on allocation (init_on_alloc)
rules.rationale: Zeroing heap allocations eliminates whole classes of uninitialized-memory info leaks and makes many use-after-free bugs unexploitable.
rules.remediation: Add init_on_alloc=1 to the kernel command line, or build with CONFIG_INIT_ON_ALLOC_DEFAULT_ON=y.

rules.title: Memory zeroed on free (init_on_free) — hardened profile
rules.rationale: Zeroing freed memory closes use-after-free info leaks and shortens the lifetime of stale secrets in the heap. Costs a few percent of performance, so it is an advisory hardened-profile control rather than a baseline requirement.
rules.remediation: Add init_on_free=1 to the kernel command line, or build with CONFIG_INIT_ON_FREE_DEFAULT_ON=y.

rules.title: Kernel built with KASLR
rules.rationale: CONFIG_RANDOMIZE_BASE randomizes the kernel load address at boot; without it every kernel address is static and predictable.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_RANDOMIZE_BASE=y (all mainstream distro kernels).

rules.title: Strong stack protector enabled
rules.rationale: Stack canaries detect and stop kernel stack buffer overflows before control-flow hijack.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_STACKPROTECTOR_STRONG=y.

rules.title: Strict kernel memory permissions
rules.rationale: CONFIG_STRICT_KERNEL_RWX makes kernel text read-only and data non-executable, blocking trivial runtime code patching.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_STRICT_KERNEL_RWX=y.

rules.title: Hardened usercopy enabled
rules.rationale: Bounds-checks copies between kernel and userspace, stopping a common heap-overflow exploitation primitive.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_HARDENED_USERCOPY=y.

rules.title: FORTIFY_SOURCE enabled
rules.rationale: Compile-time and runtime bounds checking of common string/memory functions inside the kernel.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_FORTIFY_SOURCE=y.

rules.title: SLAB freelists randomized and hardened
rules.rationale: Randomized, integrity-checked allocator freelists disrupt deterministic heap grooming used by kernel heap exploits.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_SLAB_FREELIST_RANDOM=y and CONFIG_SLAB_FREELIST_HARDENED=y.

rules.title: Lockdown LSM available
rules.rationale: The lockdown LSM prevents even root from modifying the running kernel (kexec, /dev/mem, unsigned modules), protecting kernel integrity after userspace compromise.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_SECURITY_LOCKDOWN_LSM=y.

rules.title: Unprivileged BPF off by default at build time
rules.rationale: CONFIG_BPF_UNPRIV_DEFAULT_OFF=y makes the safe sysctl value the boot default instead of relying on early userspace to set it.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_BPF_UNPRIV_DEFAULT_OFF=y.

rules.title: Direct physical memory access restricted
rules.rationale: An unrestricted /dev/mem lets root (or a compromised privileged process) read and patch arbitrary physical memory, including the kernel.
rules.remediation: Use a kernel with CONFIG_DEVMEM disabled or CONFIG_STRICT_DEVMEM=y.

rules.title: Module signature verification built in
rules.rationale: Signed modules prevent loading of tampered or attacker-built kernel modules (a classic rootkit vector).
rules.remediation: Use a kernel built with CONFIG_MODULE_SIG=y; enforce with CONFIG_MODULE_SIG_FORCE=y or module.sig_enforce=1.

rules.title: Legacy vsyscall page disabled
rules.rationale: The fixed-address legacy vsyscall page is a ready-made ROP gadget region that defeats ASLR.
rules.remediation: Use CONFIG_LEGACY_VSYSCALL_NONE=y or boot with vsyscall=none.

rules.title: No unmitigated CPU vulnerabilities
rules.rationale: Entries under /sys/devices/system/cpu/vulnerabilities reporting 'Vulnerable' indicate hardware attacks (transient execution, MDS, Retbleed, ...) that the running kernel is not mitigating.
rules.remediation: Update the kernel and CPU microcode; remove any mitigation-disabling boot parameters.

rules.title: A major LSM is active (SELinux/AppArmor/Smack/TOMOYO)
rules.rationale: Mandatory access control confines services and containers, containing the blast radius of userspace compromise. Its absence removes an entire defense layer.
rules.remediation: Enable AppArmor or SELinux for your distribution and verify with: cat /sys/kernel/security/lsm

rules.title: Yama LSM stacked
rules.rationale: Yama provides the ptrace_scope restriction; without it kernel.yama.ptrace_scope does not exist.
rules.remediation: Boot with lsm=...,yama (default on most distributions).

rules.title: Kernel lockdown mode active
rules.rationale: Lockdown in integrity or confidentiality mode stops root from modifying (or reading) the running kernel, hardening the root→kernel boundary.
rules.remediation: Boot with lockdown=integrity (typically automatic under Secure Boot).

rules.title: DCCP protocol module not loaded
rules.rationale: DCCP is an obscure, rarely-used protocol with a history of remotely-reachable kernel bugs (e.g. CVE-2017-6074 double-free LPE).
rules.remediation: Blacklist it: echo 'install dccp /bin/false' > /etc/modprobe.d/kspect-dccp.conf

rules.title: RDS protocol module not loaded
rules.rationale: RDS has produced multiple local privilege escalations (e.g. CVE-2010-3904) and is unnecessary outside Oracle RAC clusters.
rules.remediation: Blacklist it: echo 'install rds /bin/false' > /etc/modprobe.d/kspect-rds.conf

rules.title: TIPC protocol module not loaded
rules.rationale: TIPC is a niche cluster protocol with recent remotely-triggerable heap overflows (CVE-2021-43267, CVE-2022-0435).
rules.remediation: Blacklist it: echo 'install tipc /bin/false' > /etc/modprobe.d/kspect-tipc.conf

rules.title: SCTP protocol module not loaded
rules.rationale: SCTP enlarges the remotely-reachable kernel attack surface; disable it unless telecom/WebRTC workloads require it.
rules.remediation: Blacklist it: echo 'install sctp /bin/false' > /etc/modprobe.d/kspect-sctp.conf
